programs.name: Mississippi Medicaid Elderly and Disabled Waiver

eligibility.age: 21 or older (65+ or 21-64 with physical disability; those enrolled before 65 can continue after)[1][2][4]
eligibility.income_limits: Qualify as SSI beneficiary or up to 300% of SSI federal benefit rate. If exceeding 300% SSI, pay excess via income trust to Division of Medicaid. (2026 SSI federal benefit rate not specified in sources; exact dollar amounts vary annually and by household via Medicaid rules.)[2][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Standard Medicaid SSI resource limits apply (not detailed in sources). Home equity limit in 2026: $752,000 if applicant lives in home or intends to return. Exemptions: spouse lives in home; minor child (<21) or permanently disabled/blind child (any age) lives in home.[1]
other_requirements: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via in-person LTSS assessment by social worker/nurse (ADLs, IADLs, cognitive deficits scored numerically)[1][2][3][5]
Physician certification of NFLOC, re-certified every 12 months[3][5]

benefits.value: Adult day health care, home-delivered meals, personal care services, institutional respite, in-home respite, expanded/extended home health visits, community transition services, physical therapy, speech therapy, medication management, environmental safety services, case management[2][4][6][7]

methods: Contact Mississippi Division of Medicaid (DOM) via website https://medicaid.ms.gov/programs/elderly-and-disabled-waiver/ or phone (specific number not in sources; use general Medicaid intake)[2]
In-person LTSS assessment by licensed social worker/nurse[1]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; likely due to waiver caps (common for 1915(c) waivers, but unconfirmed)[null]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; LTSS assessment tool used[1][3][5]
documents: Proof of income/resources (SSI award, bank statements)
Income trust setup if over 300% SSI[1][3]

geography.regional_variations: No regional differences noted; statewide uniform administration by DOM. Providers must meet enrollment via Office of Long Term Care[2][6]
offices_or_providers: Administered by Mississippi Division of Medicaid; providers statewide per CMS-approved waiver[2]

gotchas: Must meet NFLOC via scored in-person assessment; dementia diagnosis alone insufficient[1]
Income over 300% SSI requires monthly payment via trust[3][5]
Home equity limit $752,000 in 2026 applies unless exemptions met[1]
Physician re-certification every 12 months required[3][5]
Distinguish from other MS waivers (e.g., IL for severe orthopedic/neurological, AL for assisted living)[6][7]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income capped at 300% SSI with trust option; NFLOC via scored LTSS assessment; statewide with uniform services; concurrent 1915(b)(4) selective contracting[2][4]

programs.name: Mississippi State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; prioritizes people with limited incomes but open to all Medicare beneficiaries, families, and caregivers[1][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[1]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary, under 65 with Medicare disability, dually eligible for Medicare/Medicaid, family member, or caregiver[1][5]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one counseling (in-person/phone), education, assistance with Medicare Parts A/B/C/D, Medigap, Medicare Savings Programs, Extra Help/LIS, Medicaid applications, claims/appeals, fraud prevention (SMP), printed materials, referrals, group presentations, enrollment events[1][3][5]

methods: Website: Mississippi Department of Human Services SHIP page (via shiphelp.org or mdhs.ms.gov)[5][8]
In-person: Through MDHS Division of Aging and Adult Services or local networks (contact via phone for appointment)[2][5]
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling assistance available by phone/in-person; no formal processing for enrollment as it's a service program[3][5]
forms: No specific forms required; counselors assist with Medicare/Medicaid/Extra Help applications during sessions[1][5]
documents: Medicare card, current health insurance details, medical bills/EOBs if applicable, income info if applying for savings programs (counselors guide preparation)[3][5]

geography.regional_variations: Delivered via statewide MDHS administration with local networks, affiliates, Area Agencies on Aging; no specified differences in services/wait times by region[2][5][7]
offices_or_providers: Mississippi Department of Human Services, 750 North State Street, Jackson MS 39202 (central office); local sites via community providers[2][5]

programs.official_source: https://www.mdhs.ms.gov (Mississippi Department of Human Services) or https://www.shiphelp.org/ships/mississippi/[5][8]
gotchas: Not a financial aid or healthcare provider program—only counseling/education, no direct payments or medical services; must be Medicare-related issue; prioritizes limited-income/disabled but serves all; review plans annually during open enrollment (e.g., Oct 15-Dec 7)[1][5]
Volunteers/staff handle complex appeals but cannot provide legal advice[1][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; counseling-only service via phone/in-person statewide network; prioritizes Medicare navigation and low-income program applications; administered by state aging division with local affiliates[1][2][5]

programs.name: State Long Term Care Ombudsman Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; available regardless of financial status.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; available regardless of financial status.
other_requirements: Resides in or is a potential resident of a long-term care facility subject to regulation or licensure by the Mississippi State Department of Health (e.g., nursing homes, personal care homes, assisted living facilities).

benefits.value: Advocacy to resolve complaints and problems; protection of health, safety, welfare, and rights; intervention and representation on behalf of residents; information and assistance; regular visits to facilities; community education; works to improve quality of life and care at individual, local, state, and national levels.

methods: Mail/In-person: MDHS, 200 South Lamar St., Jackson, MS 39201
application.processing_time: Timely responses to requests for information and complaints (exact timeline not specified).
documents: No specific documents required; contact ombudsman to discuss concerns or complaints.

geography.regional_variations: Delivered through contracts with Area Agencies on Aging (AAAs); 19 local ombudsmen with 2 staff statewide; may have differences in local staffing, visits, and response based on AAA regions.
offices_or_providers: Operated by Mississippi Department of Human Services, Division of Aging & Adult Services (DAAS) via AAAs across the state; main office in Jackson.

gotchas: Not a direct service provider (e.g., no financial aid, healthcare, or housing); focuses solely on advocacy and complaint resolution.
Available to potential residents (pre-admission) as well as current residents.
Anyone can file a complaint on behalf of a resident; program prioritizes resident's concerns.
Volunteers must be certified; families cannot become ombudsmen without training.
programs.data_shape_notes: no income test; open to all regardless of age or finances; advocacy-only (no direct services or benefits); delivered regionally via 19 local ombudsmen through AAAs covering all counties

programs.name: Mississippi Access to Care (MAC) Network

eligibility.income_limits: Must be Medicaid eligible; specific dollar amounts not listed in sources but follow Mississippi Medicaid ABD (Aged, Blind, Disabled) rules, which include SSI standards or institutional income limits (varies by program; e.g., for related waivers, child’s income up to institutional max in Katie Beckett). No household size table provided for MAC specifically[7][9].
eligibility.asset_limits: Follows Mississippi Medicaid rules for ABD category; home equity limit of $752,000 in 2026 if living at home (exempt if spouse or minor/permanently disabled child lives there). Standard resource limit $2,000 for some disabled programs[1].
other_requirements: Require nursing facility level of care (NFLOC), assessed in-person via ADLs, IADLs, cognitive function by licensed social worker and RN[1][7]
Live in home or have intent to return[1][7]

benefits.value: Case Management, Adult Day Services, Expanded Home Health Services, Home Delivered Meals, Personal Care Services, Institutional Respite and/or In-Home Respite, Transition Assistance[7]

methods: Phone or contact via site (specific number not listed; use Medicaid general application at medicaid.ms.gov)[7][9]
application.waitlist: Not specified; common for waiver programs due to funding caps[1]
forms: Medicaid application via medicaid.ms.gov; Pre-Admission Screening via eLTSS system for NFLOC[5]
documents: Asset documentation (e.g., home equity, bank statements)

geography.regional_variations: Delivered through local providers; NFLOC assessment may vary by assessor availability, potential regional wait differences not detailed[1][7]
offices_or_providers: Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers via mississippiaccesstocare.org[9]

gotchas: Must already be Medicaid eligible before waiver services; apply for Medicaid first[7]
NFLOC not automatic (e.g., dementia diagnosis alone insufficient; requires scored assessment)[1]
Home equity limit $752,000 applies unless exemptions met[1]
Medically stable requirement excludes unstable cases[7]
Part of broader Medicaid waiver system; slots limited, potential waitlists[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Tied to Medicaid E&D Waiver-like structure; services fixed list, eligibility hinges on NFLOC assessment score rather than simple income/age; statewide but provider-delivered

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income of no more than 125% of the federal poverty level. Exact dollar amounts vary annually by household size and are published by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services; for current figures, check the federal poverty guidelines as no Mississippi-specific table is provided in sources.
other_requirements: Enrollment priority: veterans and qualified spouses first, then individuals over 65, with disabilities, low literacy skills, limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless or at-risk, low employment prospects, or prior American Job Center users

benefits.value: Part-time community service work-based job training (average 20 hours per week) at non-profit/public facilities (e.g., schools, hospitals, senior centers); paid the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage; training and employment assistance to bridge to unsubsidized jobs.

methods: Phone or email via regional providers (see geography.offices_or_providers)
In-person or mail to provider offices (e.g., Copiah-Lincoln Community College: P.O. Box 649, Wesson, MS 39191; Phone: 601.643.8673)
Contact local partner for application (no national online form specified)
forms: Application available from local providers (e.g., contact Anna Rogers at anna.rogers@colin.edu for Copiah-Lincoln)
documents: Proof of age (55+), unemployment status, family income (≤125% federal poverty level), and priority factors (e.g., veteran status, disability documentation)

geography.restrictions: Limited to counties served by specific providers; not all Mississippi counties covered (e.g., excludes some northern counties)
geography.regional_variations: Administered by multiple local providers with different offices, contacts, and potentially varying wait times or host sites by region/cluster of counties.
offices_or_providers: City of Jackson Senior Services Division (Hinds, Rankin, Scott, Warren Counties): Kimberly Moore, (601) 960-0419, kimberlym@city.jackson.ms.us

Copiah-Lincoln Community College (Adams, Amite, Claiborne, Copiah, Franklin, Jefferson, Lawrence, Lincoln, Pike, Simpson, Smith, Walthall, Wilkinson Counties): Brenda Orr, (601) 643-8671, brenda.orr@colin.edu
East Central Planning and Development District (Clarke, Jasper, Kemper, Lauderdale, Neshoba Counties): Rosie Coleman, (601) 683-2007, rjcoleman@ecpdd.org

Southern Mississippi Planning and Development District (Forrest, George, Greene, Hancock, Harrison, Jackson, Jones, Lamar, Marion, Pearl River, Perry, Stone, Wayne Counties): Jan Hale, (228) 314-1433, jhale@smpdd.com
Southern Foundation for Homeless Children, Inc. (Pearl River, Jones, Harrison, Jefferson Davis, Forrest, Covington Counties)

gotchas: Not statewide—check if your county is served; contact provider if not listed
Must be unemployed and actively seeking unsubsidized work; part-time training role only
Priority tiers limit access for non-priority applicants
Income based on 125% federal poverty level (updates yearly; verify current thresholds)
No asset limits mentioned, but prove low-income status required
programs.data_shape_notes: County-restricted with multiple regional providers; coverage clustered by planning districts; no statewide single office or online application; benefits fixed at ~20 hours/week at min wage
